title: The Murrumbidgee Shearer
content:
혻혻혻혻Come, all you jolly natives, and I占퐇l relate to you
혻혻혻혻Some of my observations占폸dventures, too, a few.
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e travelled about the country for miles, full many a score,
혻혻혻혻And oft-times would have hungered, but for the cheek I bore.

혻혻혻혻I占퐒e coasted on the Barwon占퐇ow down the Darling, too,
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e been on the Murrumbidgee, and out on the Paroo;
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e been on all the diggings, boys, from famous Ballarat;
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e loafed upon the Lachlan and fossicked Lambing Flat.

혻혻혻혻I went up to a squatter, and asked him for a feed,
혻혻혻혻But the knowledge of my hunger was swallowed by his greed.
혻혻혻혻He said I was a loafer and for work had no desire,
혻혻혻혻And so, to do him justice, I set his shed on fire.

혻혻혻혻Oh, yes, I占퐒e touched the shepherd占퐏 hut, of sugar, tea, and flour;
혻혻혻혻And a tender bit of mutton I always could devour.
혻혻혻혻I went up to a station, and there I got a job;
혻혻혻혻Plunged in the store, and hooked it, with a very tidy lob.

혻혻혻혻Oh, yes, my jolly dandies, I占퐒e done it on the cross.
혻혻혻혻Although I carry bluey now, I占퐒e sweated many a horse.
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e helped to ease the escort of many占퐏 the ounce of gold;
혻혻혻혻The traps have often chased me, more times than can be told.

혻혻혻혻Oh, yes, the traps have chased me, been frightened of their stripes
혻혻혻혻They never could have caught me, they feared my cure for gripes.
혻혻혻혻And well they knew I carried it, which they had often seen
혻혻혻혻A-glistening in my flipper, chaps, a patent pill machine.

혻혻혻혻I占퐒e been hunted like a panther into my mountain lair.
혻혻혻혻Anxiety and misery my grim companions there.
혻혻혻혻I占퐒e planted in the scrub, my boys, and fed on kangaroo,
혻혻혻혻And wound up my avocations by ten years on Cockatoo.

혻혻혻혻So you can understand, my boys, just from this little rhyme,
혻혻혻혻I占퐉 a Murrumbidgee shearer, and one of the good old time.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
